frameworks.name: utility maximization
frameworks.one_line: Choices reflect maximization of an underlying subjective value representation

frameworks.name: Generalized Axiom of Revealed Preference
frameworks.one_line: Internal-consistency test used to diagnose utility maximization from choice

frameworks.name: value-based decision making
frameworks.one_line: Framework in which options are selected by assigned subjective values

frameworks.name: dopaminergic reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: Learned value updates require intact dopamine signaling

core_question: Can a nervous system as small as C. elegans choose between foods as if maximizing utility?
key_assumptions: GARP is necessary and sufficient to certify utility maximization from choice data
Subjective values are assigned to food options and can be learned
Identified chemosensory neurons can implement value assignment
manipulations: Offered bacterial food options of distinct growth potentials
Manipulated prior consumption to test learning of food values
Perturbed dopamine signaling to test its role in value learning
Combined microfluidic choice assay with electrophysiology of chemosensory neurons
key_findings: C. elegans food choices satisfy necessary and sufficient conditions for utility maximization
Choices are well-fit by a utility function used to model human consumers
Value learning in C. elegans requires intact dopamine signaling
Chemosensory neuron responses to foods are amplified by prior consumption
interpretations: Nematodes behave as if they maintain and maximize a representation of subjective value
Identified chemosensory neurons may form part of a value-assignment system
